mergers.merger_description:
GIP V Snow Bidco Limited (the **Acquirer**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Envol Holdings Limited and its subsidiaries including TCR Group (the **Target**) (the **Acquisition**).

The Acquirer is a special purpose vehicle controlled by funds managed and/or advised by Global Infrastructure Management, LLC (together with is affiliates, **Global Infrastructure Management**).

Global Infrastructure Management is the global independent infrastructure fund manager of Global Infrastructure Partners (**GIP**), headquartered in New York, United States (**US**), and primarily focused on investing in transportation, energy, waste, water, and digital infrastructure sectors. Global Infrastructure Management also holds a 37% interest in Sydney Aviation Alliance Holdings Pty Ltd, which fully owns Sydney Airport Corporation Ltd. Global Infrastructure Management is ultimately controlled by BlackRock, Inc. (**BlackRock**).

BlackRock is a US company listed on the New York Stock Exchange. BlackRock is active in the provision of global investment management, risk management and advisory services to institutional and retail clients around the world.

The Target is the holding entity of TCR Group (**TCR**), which is primarily active in full-service leasing of ground support equipment (**GSE**) to independent ground handlers, airlines, and airports globally. TCR also provides repair and maintenance services for GSE (on both leased and third-party fleet) and fleet management services. TCR is headquartered in Steenokkerzeel, Belgium.

In Australia, TCR is involved in the leasing out and maintenance of GSE at airports around Australia through various subsidiaries.

TCR is currently indirectly solely controlled by 3i Group plc. 3i Group plc is an international investment company listed on the London Stock Exchange, with offices in Europe and North America.

events.title: Global Infrastructure Management - TCR – Phase 1 determination

determination_table_content.details: GIP V Snow Bidco Limited proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Envol Holdings Limited and its subsidiaries including TCR Group.
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, GIP V Snow Bidco Limited (the Acquirer) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Envol Holdings Limited and its subsidiaries including TCR Group (the Target) (the Acquisition).

The Acquirer is a special purpose vehicle controlled by funds managed and/or advised by Global Infrastructure Management, LLC (together with is affiliates, Global Infrastructure Management).

Global Infrastructure Management is the global independent infrastructure fund manager of Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP), headquartered in New York, United States (US), and primarily focused on investing in transportation, energy, waste, water, and digital infrastructure sectors. Relevantly to the Acquisition, Global Infrastructure Management also holds a 37% interest in Sydney Aviation Alliance Holdings Pty Ltd, which fully owns Sydney Airport Corporation Ltd. (Sydney Airport). Sydney Airport owns small amounts of GSE with which it provides services to airlines. Global Infrastructure Management is ultimately controlled by BlackRock, Inc. (BlackRock).

BlackRock is a US company listed on the New York Stock Exchange.

BlackRock is active in the provision of global investment management, risk management and advisory services to institutional and retail clients around the world.

The target is the holding entity of TCR Group (TCR), which is primarily active in full-service leasing of ground support equipment (GSE) to independent ground handlers, airlines, and airports globally.

TCR also provides repair and maintenance services for GSE (on both leased and third-party fleet) and fleet management services. TCR is headquartered in Steenokkerzeel, Belgium.

In Australia, TCR is involved in the leasing out and maintenance of GSE at airports around Australia through various subsidiaries.

TCR is currently indirectly solely controlled by 3i Group plc. 3i Group plc is an international investment company listed on the London Stock Exchange, with offices in Europe and North America.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details: There are vertical relationships between Global Infrastructure Management and TCR relating to services at Sydney Airport. The parties also overlap in the supply of GSE services to airlines at Sydney Airport.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form, information submitted by third parties and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• Noting a concern was raised by a third party that the Acquisition may reduce competitors’ access to space at Sydney Airport, the merged firm would be unlikely to have the ability to foreclose rivals’ access to inputs at Sydney Airport as it does not have sufficient control over the entities that ultimately control Sydney Airport so as to foreclose rivals’ access and Sydney Airport would not have the incentive to foreclose rivals’ access.

• The merged firm would likely continue to be constrained by alternative suppliers of GSE services.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

Restaurant Brands New Zealand Limited (**Restaurant Brands**) and Yum! Brands, Inc. (**Yum**) propose to acquire, via a joint venture (Taco Ventures Pty Ltd (**JV Co**)):

* 9 Taco Bell restaurants from Restaurant Brands’ subsidiary QSR Pty Ltd, and

Restaurant Brands will control JV Co and hold a 60% interest, while Gardiner Lane Capital, LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of Yum, will acquire a 40% interest.

Yum holds equity in and operates around 70 KFC restaurants in Australia but does not operate or hold equity in any Taco Bell or Pizza Hut restaurants in Australia.

Restaurant Brands is a Yum corporate franchisee. In Australia, it operates 9 Taco Bell restaurants, all of which are in NSW, and 74 KFC restaurants.

Collins is an ASX-listed Australian fast-food corporation that owns and operates 294 KFC restaurants and 20 Taco Bell restaurants in Australia, under franchise arrangements with Yum.

CVS Vets (Australia) Proprietary Limited, which is part of CVS Group Plc (together, **CVS Group**), proposes to acquire 60% of the shares in Newman Vet Pty Ltd (trading as Paws at Prospect Vet and Eyes at Prospect Vet) (**Paws**), a small animal veterinary practice in Adelaide (the **Acquisition**). The existing owners of Paws will retain the remaining 40% of shares in Paws.

CVS Group is an integrated veterinary services provider based in the United Kingdom and listed on the London Stock Exchange. In Australia, CVS Group owns a number of small animal veterinary practices in Queensland, New South Wales, the Australian Capital Territory, Victoria, South Australia and Western Australia. CVS Group currently owns three veterinary practices in Adelaide: Walkerville Vet, Pet Universe Manningham and Pet Universe Northgate.

CVS Group and Paws both supply small animal veterinary services in Adelaide. Small animal veterinary practices attend to small animals (such as cats, dogs, guinea pigs, birds, rabbits and other pets), rather than larger animals (such as cattle, sheep and horses), and offer services such as consultation, general and preventative health care/advice, surgery, microchipping, dentistry, radiology, pathology, pharmacy and related services such as puppy school.

In addition to first opinion small animal vet services, Paws also provides specialist ophthalmic vet services on referral from first opinion vets. CVS does not own any specialist vet services clinics in South Australia.

mergers.merger_name: Opal Healthcare – Sunnymeade Park Aged Care Community

targets.name: The assets and business comprising Sunnymeade Park Aged Care Community

mergers.merger_description:
DPG Services Pty Ltd (**DPG**) and OHCA Property Holdings Pty Limited (being related bodies corporate, and together referred to as **Opal Healthcare**) propose to acquire the land, assets and business comprising the Sunnymeade Park Aged Care Community (**Sunnymeade Park**) (**Acquisition**).

DPG is a registered provider of approved residential care homes, and owns and operates 144 residential aged care homes across New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, Western Australia and South Australia.

Sunnymeade Park is a privately-owned, single approved residential care home with 172 beds, located at 362-376 King Street, Caboolture in Queensland. Sunnymeade Park provides the services required under the *Aged Care Act 2024 (Cth)*, including 24/7 nursing care, ageing in place, secure dementia areas and palliative care. The sellers are Jomal Pty Ltd and Margaret Jean Walker, the latter also as Executor of the estate of Leslie Kenneth Walker and Margaret Jean Walker, being partners of the Leslie K & Margaret J Walker Partnership.

The Acquisition gives rise to a competitive overlap between the parties’ activities in South-East Queensland.

events.title: Opal Healthcare - Sunnymeade Park Aged Care Community - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: DPG Services Pty Ltd and OHCA Property Holdings Pty Limited (being related bodies corporate, and together referred to as Opal Healthcare) propose to acquire the land, assets and business comprising the Sunnymeade Park Aged Care Community (Sunnymeade Park) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Opal Healthcare, owns and operates 144 residential aged care facilities across New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, Western Australia and South Australia. It provides accommodation and services for residents, including physiotherapy and wellness programs, dementia care, respite care and palliative care. Relevant to the Acquisition, Opal Healthcare owns and operates 21 residential care facilities in Queensland, including 7 in the Brisbane metropolitan area, 3 on the Sunshine Coast, 7 on the Gold Coast and 4 located between the north of Brisbane and the Sunshine Coast.

Sunnymeade Park is a privately-owned, single-approved residential aged care facility with 172 beds, located in Caboolture, Queensland.

Sunnymeade Park provides the services required under the Aged Care Act 2024 (Cth) as an approved residential care home, including 24/7 nursing care, ageing in place, secure dementia areas and palliative care. The sellers are Jomal Pty Ltd and Margaret Jean Walker, the latter also as Executor of the estate of Leslie Kenneth Walker and Margaret Jean Walker, being partners of the Leslie K & Margaret J Walker Partnership.

Opal Healthcare and Sunnymeade Park overlap in the supply of residential aged care facilities in South-East Queensland.

However, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has not reached a concluded view on market definition given that, based on the information available and for the reasons below, it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market whether a narrow or broad geographic market dimension is adopted.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the ACCC undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• The aggregation arising from the Acquisition is estimated to be low at a local and regional level (as well as at the national level) given the number of beds at Sunnymeade Park and the total number of beds available in the relevant areas.

• Opal Healthcare would likely continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of residential aged care facilities including from large national providers such as Bolton Clarke and Regis which have facilities in the local area.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_name: Ramsay Health Care – National Capital Private Hospital

acquirers.name: RAMSAY HEALTH CARE AUSTRALIA PTY LIMITED
RAMSAY HEALTH CARE INVESTMENTS PTY LIMITED

mergers.merger_description:
Ramsay Health Care Investments (Ramsay) or one or more of its wholly owned subsidiaries proposes to acquire the businesses, assets and certain assumed liabilities of National Capital Private Hospital (NCPH) from Healthscope pursuant to a Business and Asset Sale Agreement.

Ramsay is a publicly listed private hospital operator with over 75 hospitals, clinics and surgical centres across Australia. Ramsay does not currently have any facilities in the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) or its surrounding regions. The closest Ramsay operated private hospitals to NCPH are Southern Highlands Private Hospital in Bowral, New South Wales (NSW) and Nowra Private Hospital in Nowra, NSW.

NCPH is a private hospital in Garran, ACT which is currently operated by Healthscope, a private hospital operator with 36 hospitals Australia-wide. In May 2025, Healthscope’s parent entities went into voluntary administration and receivers and administrators were appointed by its lenders and the Healthscope Board, respectively, to conduct an orderly sale of the hospitals in its network.

Private hospitals supply services to patients, private health funds in respect of their members and to government for Medicare patients. Private hospital operators also supply facilities to medical and allied health professionals providing services within the hospitals that they operate.

events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: The proposed acquisition by Ramsay Health Care Investments Pty Limited (Ramsay) or one or more of its wholly owned subsidiaries of the businesses, assets and certain assumed liabilities of National Capital Private Hospital (National Capital) from Healthscope pursuant to a Business and Asset Sale Agreement (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Ramsay, is a publicly listed private hospital operator with over 75 hospitals, clinics and surgical centres across Australia.

The target, National Capital, is a private hospital in Garran, ACT which is currently operated by Healthscope, a private hospital operator with 36 hospitals Australia-wide.

In May 2025, Healthscope’s parent entities went into voluntary administration, and receivers and administrators were appointed by its lenders and the Healthscope Board, respectively, to conduct an orderly sale of the hospitals in its network.

Ramsay is a private hospital operator and National Capital is a private hospital located in Garran, ACT.

Ramsay does not currently have any facilities in the Australian Capital Territory (ACT) or its surrounding regions. The closest Ramsay operated private hospitals to National Capital are Southern Highlands Private Hospital in Bowral, New South Wales (NSW) and Nowra Private Hospital in Nowra, NSW, both of which are more than 150km drive from National Capital.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and information from third parties.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings:

• there is limited horizontal overlap between National Capital and Ramsay-operated private hospitals, as these hospitals have largely different geographical catchment zones for patients, and limited overlap of Visiting Medical Officers (VMOs). Accordingly, the Acquisition is not likely to enable Ramsay to profitably worsen either non-price terms to patients, or price or non-price terms to VMOs serving the Canberra region; and

• the Acquisition is not likely to substantially alter the competitive dynamics between Ramsay and private health insurers, such that there would be a substantial lessening of competition in the supply of Hospital Purchaser Provider Agreements (HPPAs) to private health insurers, enabling Ramsay to profitably increase the prices or worsen the non-price terms of its HPPAs with private health insurers, either in relation to Ramsay’s existing hospitals or National Capital itself.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description: Parker-Hannifin Corporation (**Parker-Hannifin**) proposes to acquire 100% of the share capital in Filtration Group Corporation (**Filtration Group**). Parker-Hannifin is a global engineering company headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio, USA. It designs, manufactures and supplies motion and control technologies and systems to a variety of industries, including aerospace and defence, transportation, energy, and HVAC (heating, ventilation and air conditioning) and refrigeration. Filtration Group is a global filtration and separation technology company headquartered in Illinois, USA. Filtration Group’s core business is the design, manufacture and supply of filtration and separation products for life sciences, indoor air quality and industrial applications. Parker-Hannifin and Filtration Group both supply filtration products for use in industrial applications in Australia.

events.title: Parker-Hannifin - Filtration Group - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: Parker-Hannifin Corporation (Parker-Hannifin)’s proposed acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Filtration Group Corporation (Filtration Group) (together, the Parties) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Parker-Hannifin, is a global engineering company headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio, USA. It designs, manufactures and supplies motion and control technologies and systems to a variety of industries, including aerospace and defence, transportation, energy, and HVAC (heating, ventilation and air conditioning) and refrigeration.

The target, Filtration Group, is a global filtration and separation technology company headquartered in Illinois, USA. Filtration Group’s core business is the design, manufacture and supply of filtration and separation products for life sciences, indoor air quality and industrial applications.

In Australia, Parker-Hannifin and Filtration Group overlap in the supply of filtration products for use in industrial applications in the following categories: (a) Filter cartridges used in the processing of oil and gas (b) Filters for hydraulic fluid systems (c) Process water filtration products (d) Condensate drains used for compressed air systems.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and information from third parties.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market in Australia. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• The Parties would continue to face competition from alternative large, established suppliers of filtration products in Australia, as well as many smaller competitors.

• Filtration products are readily interchangeable and built to standardised specifications making it easy for customers to switch brands.

• Filtration products are not generally purchased under long-term supply agreements, meaning customers are able to switch suppliers with relative ease.

• Filtration Group is a relatively small competitor in the supply of filtration products in Australia, so there will be a small increase in the market share of the combined entity.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_name: Charter Hall Holdings - JGS Private Capital

anzsic_codes.name: Other Auxiliary Finance and Investment Services

mergers.merger_description:
Charter Hall Holdings Pty Limited (**Charter Hall Holdings**) is wholly owned by Charter Hall Limited (ASX: CHC) and proposes to acquire 100% of JGS Private Capital Pty Ltd (**JGS Private Capital**), which is wholly owned by J.G. Service Pty Limited (the **Proposed Acquisition**).

JGS Private Capital operates an investment and property management business and serves as trustee of 8 unit trusts, with 7 of the units holding interests in commercial real estate and one being a finance trust (**Trusts**). CareSuper Pty Ltd (**CareSuper**) is the sole unitholder of each Trust.

Charter Hall Holdings operates a property investment and management business and serves as trustee of land-holding trusts.

Both Charter Hall Holdings and JGS Private Capital are involved in leasing and/or licensing commercial real estate to existing or prospective tenants to generate rental returns.

CareSuper is JGS Private Capital’s only client, and is also an existing client of Charter Hall Holdings, but JGS Private Capital and Charter Hall Holdings are otherwise unrelated.

There are no vertical relationships between Charter Hall Holdings and JGS Private Capital.

Through the Proposed Acquisition Charter Hall Holdings will acquire the right to manage and control the Trusts and their underlying assets. JGS Private Capital owns no beneficial ownership interest in any Trust or any of the real property assets held by the Trusts, which will continue following completion. Beneficial ownership of the Trusts and their underlying assets will remain with CareSuper following completion of the transaction.

events.title: Charter Hall Holdings - JGS Private Capital - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Charter Hall Holdings Pty Limited (Charter Hall Holdings) is wholly owned by Charter Hall Limited (ASX:CHC) and proposes to acquire 100% of JGS Private Capital Pty Ltd (JGS Private Capital), which is wholly owned by J.G. Service Pty Limited (the Proposed Acquisition).

JGS Private Capital operates an investment and property management business and serves as trustee of 8 unit trusts, with 7 of the units holding interests in commercial real estate and 1 being a finance trust (Trusts). CareSuper Pty Ltd (CareSuper) is the sole unitholder of each Trust.

Through the Proposed Acquisition, Charter Hall Holdings will acquire the right to manage and control the Trusts and their underlying assets. JGS Private Capital has no beneficial ownership interest in any Trust or any of the real property assets held by the Trusts, which will continue following completion. Beneficial ownership of the Trusts and their underlying assets will remain with CareSuper following completion of the transaction.
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Charter Hall Holdings, is an Australian property investment and funds management group. Charter Hall Holdings is involved in leasing and/or licensing commercial real estate to existing or prospective tenants to generate investment returns. This may include incidental services such as property maintenance.

The target, JGS Private Capital, operates an investment and property management business and serves as trustee of the Trusts.

CareSuper is the sole unitholder of each Trust.

Charter Hall Holdings and JGS Private Capital (together, the Parties) overlap in the supply of commercial real estate investment services and the supply of commercial real estate leasing services.

Commercial real estate investment services include investment management services (asset acquisition, divestments, restructuring and management for the purpose of generating a financial return to investors) and property management services (leasing, maintenance, development and associated activities in connection with the management and operation of Australian commercial real estate assets). These services facilitate passive income generation and capital growth for investors located across Australia, and function via commercial real estate assets held and managed by the JGS Private Capital and Charter Hall Holdings.

In respect of investment services, CareSuper is JGS Private Capital’s only client, and is also an existing client of Charter Hall Holdings.

JGS Private Capital and Charter Hall Holdings are otherwise unrelated.

Commercial real estate leasing services may include incidental services such as maintaining properties and dealing with lease expiries and extensions, and are for the purpose of generating investment returns for investors in commercial real estate assets.

In respect of leasing services, the Parties overlap in Canberra, Melbourne and Sydney.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• JGS Private Capital’s portfolio of assets under management is relatively small in comparison to Charter Hall Holdings and other suppliers of commercial real estate investment and leasing services.

• Charter Hall Holdings’ market share aggregation in respect of the supply of commercial real estate investment and leasing services is estimated to be low.

• After the Proposed Acquisition, Charter Hall Holdings would likely continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of commercial real estate investment and leasing services, such as Goodman Group, Lendlease, Dexus, GPT Group and Mirvac.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_name: Opal HealthCare – Greenwich Place residential aged care home
mergers.merger_description:
DPG Services Pty Ltd (**DPG**) and OHCA Property Holdings Pty Ltd (**OHCA**) (being related bodies corporate, and together referred to as Opal Healthcare) propose to acquire the land, assets and business comprising the Greenwich Place residential aged care home located in Greenwich, Sydney (**Greenwich Place**) (**Acquisitio**n).

DPG is a registered provider of approved residential care homes and owns and operates 145 residential aged care homes across New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland, Western Australia and South Australia.

Greenwich Place is a privately-owned, single approved residential care home with 90 beds, located at 33 Greenwich Rd, Greenwich in New South Wales. Greenwich Place provides the services required under the Aged Care Act 2024 (Cth), including permanent care, respite care and palliative care.

The Acquisition will involve:

* DPG acquiring the assets comprising the Greenwich Place business from Greenwich Place Pty Ltd, Greenwich Care Pty Limited and Stamen Greenwich Property Ltd; and
* OHCA acquiring the land on which Greenwich Place is operated from Stamen Greenwich Property Pty Ltd.

There is a competitive overlap between Opal Healthcare’s activities and the activities of the target, Greenwich Place, in Sydney.

Sales enablement software platforms help improve enterprise sales teams’ performance by providing document management and collaboration capabilities, data analytics capabilities and learning and coaching modules to build sales skills. mergers.merger_description:
Fulton Hogan Industries Pty Ltd (**Fulton Hogan**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Avionics Holdings Pty Ltd representing the Australian operations of the Avionics Group business (**Avionics**) (the **Acquisition**).

Fulton Hogan is a civil construction and infrastructure provider with primary operations in Australia and New Zealand. It delivers projects and long‑term maintenance across airports, bridges, communications, energy, rail, roads and water sectors. Relevantly, Fulton Hogan’s airfield works operations primarily involve airfield pavement construction services such as runway resurfacing, taxiway upgrades and apron rehabilitation. Fulton Hogan typically operates as head contractor, whereby it also coordinates the delivery of projects and subcontracts third parties to complete constituent components of the project, including aeronautical ground lighting (**AGL**). Occasionally, Fulton Hogan will be appointed to deliver airfield pavement and construction services on projects where it is not the head contractor.

AGL is a specialised system of in-ground and above-ground lights and other visual aids used to guide pilots during take-off, landing and taxiing. Avionics supplies and installs AGL as part of airside works projects in Australia. Avionics also provides related services such as design, repair and maintenance of AGL. Avionics and other AGL providers either supply these services directly to airfield operators or to head contractors which coordinate delivery of major airfield works.

mergers.merger_description:
Bain Capital Investors, LLC (**Bain Capital)** proposes to indirectly acquire 100% of the shares in MCJ Co., Ltd. (**MCJ**) through a newly created special purpose vehicle, BCPE Meta Cayman, L.P.

Bain Capital is a limited liability company organised in Delaware under United States law and is the ultimate general partner of numerous private equity funds. Bain Capital, through such funds, invests in companies with activities in various industries, including technology, media and telecommunications, healthcare, retail and consumer products, financial and business services, and industrial/manufacturing.

MCJ is listed on the Tokyo Stock Exchange. MCJ is an integrated Information and Communications Technology business that operates predominantly through its group companies that are primarily active in Japan. In Australia, the only services that MCJ supplies are IT product repair and support services through its brand R-Logic.

determination_table_content.details: Bain Capital Investors, LLC (Bain Capital) proposes to indirectly acquire 100% of the shares in MCJ Co., Ltd. (MCJ) through a newly created special purpose vehicle, BCPE Meta Cayman, L.P. (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Bain Capital, is the entity responsible for managing numerous private equity funds. These funds invest in companies with activities in various industries, including technology, media and telecommunications, healthcare, retail and consumer products, financial and business services, and industrial/manufacturing.

The companies that generate revenue in Australia and are active in technology, media and telecommunications are:

• CentralSquare Technologies – a provider of public sector software

• Citius Tech – a provider of consulting and digital technology to healthcare and life sciences companies

• EnterpriseDB – a provider of relational database management systems

• Envestnet – a provider of integrated wealth technology software and solutions to financial advisors and institutions.

The goods and services supplied by these companies do not overlap with the goods and services supplied by the target, MCJ.

MCJ, is an integrated Information and Communications Technology business that operates primarily through its group companies that are predominantly active in Japan.

In Australia, the only services that MCJ supplies are IT product repair and support services through its brand R-Logic which supplies one Australian customer.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details: There is no horizontal overlap or vertical relationship between Bain Capital and MCJ.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings:

• there is no horizontal overlap or vertical relationship between Bain Capital and MCJ in Australia.

mergers.merger_description:
Audiotonix Limited (Audiotonix) intends to acquire 100% of the shares and sole control of Soundtech Group S.r.l. (Soundtech Group) through 2 special purpose vehicles, Sound Bidco S.r.l and Sound Bidco (Denmark) ApS. Both special purpose vehicles will be 100% directly owned by A6 Audio Bidco Limited (A6 Audio), which, in turn, is 100% indirectly owned by Audiotonix. A6 Audio and Audiotonix are part of Audiotonix Group, a UK-headquartered group active in the design, engineering and manufacturing of professional audio mixing consoles, as well as associated equipment such as networking (allowing consoles to connect with other consoles), interface products (used to physically control music editing and mixing remotely from a console), software (e.g., audio processing, control software and operating systems) and spare parts.

Audiotonix is under the sole control of PAI Partners, a private equity firm with a focus on investing in business services, food and consumer, general industrials and healthcare.

In Australia, Audiotonix’s subsidiary Sound Devices is a wholesale supplier of wireless microphone systems.

Soundtech Group is a supplier in the professional audio industry which is active in the design, manufacturing and application of micro-electronic mechanisms.

Soundtech Group’s subsidiaries include DPA Microphones A/S (DPA Microphones), Austrian Audio GmbH (Austrian Audio) and Wisycom S.r.l. (Wisycom).

In Australia, DPA Microphones and Austrian Audio are wholesale suppliers of high-end microphones, and Wisycom is a wholesale supplier of wireless microphone systems and wireless in-ear monitoring systems.

determination_table_content.details: Audiotonix Limited (Audiotonix)’s proposed acquisition, via Sound Bidco S.r.l. and Sound Bidco (Denmark) ApS, of 100% of the shares in Soundtech Group S.r.l. (Soundtech Group) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Audiotonix, is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Audiotonix Group Limited (Audiotonix Group), an UK-headquartered group active in the design, engineering and manufacturing of professional audio mixing consoles, associated equipment such as networking (allowing consoles to connect with other consoles), interface products (used to physically control music editing and mixing remotely from a console), software (e.g., audio processing, control software and operating systems) and spare parts. Audiotonix Group is ultimately owned by PAI Partners S.à r.l. (PAI Partners), an independent private equity firm headquartered in Paris, France. PAI Partners invests in business services, food and consumer, general industrials and healthcare.

In Australia, Audiotonix’s subsidiary Sound Devices LLC (Sound Devices) is a wholesale supplier of portable and installed production sound and video products for professional applications, including wireless microphone systems.

The target, Soundtech Group, is a supplier of professional audio micro-electronic mechanisms. Its subsidiaries include DPA Microphones A/S (DPA Microphones), Austrian Audio GmbH (Austrian Audio) and Wisycom S.r.l. (Wisycom).

In Australia, DPA Microphones and Austrian Audio are wholesale suppliers of high-end microphones, and Wisycom is a wholesale supplier of wireless microphone systems and wireless in-ear monitoring systems.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Audiotonix and Soundtech Group (together, the Parties) overlap in the supply of wireless microphone systems in Australia. Soundtech Group also supplies wireless in-ear monitoring systems in Australia.

Wireless microphone systems refer to audio solutions for use on stage, on set or on the move that combine microphones, wireless transmitters and receivers. Sound is recorded through a wireless handheld microphone or through other types of microphones, such as on-body or on-clothing microphones, that communicates with a bodypack transmitter. The sound is transmitted to a wireless receiver, which can be connected to various other microphones/transmitters, and transfers the recorded sound to other sound systems for processing. Antennas can be used as part of the system to pick up radio frequency-transmitted sound over longer distances.

Wireless in-ear monitoring systems refer to audio solutions used in live, broadcast and theatrical environments and enable different performers to hear all or relevant parts of the sound of the performance. These systems combine wireless transmitters, receivers worn on the body by the performers which transmit the sound to an audio device, such as ear moulds, and combiners that combine radio frequency outputs from several transmitters into a single antenna.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• The Parties’ market share aggregation is estimated to be low on any market definition, with an estimated post-acquisition market share of below 10%.

• The Parties would continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of wireless microphone and in-ear monitoring systems in Australia, such as Sennheiser, Shure, Audio-Technica and Lectrosonics.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its indirect wholly-owned subsidiary Project Saint Merger Sub, Inc., proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular, Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).

Medtronic is a global healthcare technology company incorporated in Ireland and listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Globally (including in Australia), Medtronic develops, manufactures, distributes and sells device-based medical therapies and services within its cardiovascular, neuroscience and medical surgery portfolios. In Australia, Medtronic operates through its Australian subsidiary, Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd, to supply medical devices, including neurovascular devices such as guidewires, catheters, aspiration catheters, embolic coils, flow diverters, liquid embolic agents, intrasaccular devices, AB stents and stent retrievers. In relation to the supply of guidewires in Australia, Medtronic supplies its Mirage .008” guidewire.

Scientia is a medical device company headquartered in West Valley City, Utah, USA. Scientia specialises in microfabrication technology for guidewires and catheters used in neurovascular and peripheral vascular procedures. In Australia, Scientia supplies guidewires under its “Aristotle” brand through a third-party distributor, LifeHealthcare Pty Ltd. The Aristotle-branded guidewires are supplied in Australia in four diameter configurations, .014”, .018”, .024”, and .035”.

Both Medtronic and Scientia supply neurovascular guidewires in Australia.

events.title: Medtronic - Scientia - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: Medtronic plc (Medtronic), via its wholly owned subsidiary United States Surgical Corporation, proposes to acquire Scientia Vascular, Inc. (Scientia) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Medtronic, is a global healthcare technology company incorporated in Ireland and listed on the New York Stock Exchange.

Globally (including in Australia), Medtronic develops, manufactures, distributes and sells device-based medical therapies and services within its cardiovascular, neuroscience and medical surgery portfolios.

In Australia, Medtronic operates through its Australian subsidiary, Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd, to supply medical devices, including neurovascular devices such as guidewires, catheters, aspiration catheters, embolic coils, flow diverters, liquid embolic agents, intrasaccular devices, AB stents and stent retrievers. In relation to the supply of guidewires in Australia, Medtronic supplies its Mirage .008” guidewire.

The target, Scientia, is a medical device company headquartered in West Valley City, Utah, USA. Scientia specialises in microfabrication technology for guidewires and catheters used in neurovascular and peripheral vascular procedures.

In Australia, Scientia supplies guidewires under its “Aristotle” brand through a third-party distributor, LifeHealthcare Pty Ltd. The Aristotle-branded guidewires are supplied in Australia in four diameter configurations, .014”, .018”, .024”, and .035”.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details: Medtronic and Scientia both supply neurovascular guidewires in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form, information from third parties and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• For the supply of neurovascular guidewires in Australia, the market share aggregation resulting from the Acquisition is estimated to be low, and post-Acquisition, the merged entity will continue to face competition from multiple alternative suppliers of neurovascular guidewires.

• Some market participants were concerned that the Acquisition could give rise to conglomerate effects by enabling Medtronic to bundle its existing neurovascular medical devices with Scientia’s guidewires, resulting in foreclosure of rival guidewire suppliers.

• The ACCC has had regard to: 1. the position of the Parties in their respective markets, including Medtronic’s position as a supplier of a portfolio of neurovascular medical devices, 2. the presence of alternative suppliers of both neurovascular guidewires and other neurovascular medical devices, and 3. customer procurement processes for neurovascular medical devices.

• The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to conglomerate effects, as the merged entity would be unlikely to have the ability and incentive to engage in various commercial arrangements (such as offering bulk or multi-product discounts) to the point of foreclosing rival neurovascular guidewire suppliers and harming competition in the supply of neurovascular guidewires.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (ServiceNow) proposes to acquire Veza Technologies, Inc. (Veza).

ServiceNow is a US software company. It supplies products which automate and simplify manual tasks to facilitate autonomous, coordinated workflows for organisations. These products can be applied across a range of operations including customer workflows (e.g. for order fulfilment), technology workflows (e.g. for IT and risk management and security operations), core business workflows (e.g. for HR employee onboarding) and creator workflows (workflows to support software developers). These services are delivered on ServiceNow’s ‘Now Platform’. ServiceNow also supplies IT security software to organisations via that platform.

Veza is a US security company that supplies Identity Access Management products, which are designed to enable companies to manage access to their data. These products are delivered via Veza’s ‘Access Platform’, which allows companies to monitor privilege access, investigate identity threats, automate access reviews, and introduce access governance to enterprise resources. Veza supplies identity governance and administration solutions, which are solutions that establish and manage the rules and processes governing access to systems. Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.

events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: ServiceNow, Inc. (ServiceNow)’s proposed acquisition of 100% of the issued shares in Veza Technologies, Inc. (Veza) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

ServiceNow is a US software company. It supplies products which automate and simplify manual tasks to facilitate autonomous, coordinated workflows for organisations. These products can be applied across a range of operations including customer workflows (e.g. for order fulfilment), technology workflows (e.g. for IT and risk management and security operations), core business workflows (e.g. for HR employee onboarding) and creator workflows (workflows to support software developers). These services are delivered on ServiceNow’s ‘Now Platform’. ServiceNow also supplies IT security software to organisations via that platform.

The target, Veza, is a US security company that supplies Identity Access Management (IAM) products, which are designed to enable companies to manage access to their data. These products are delivered via Veza’s ‘Access Platform’, which allows companies to monitor privilege access, investigate identity threats, automate access reviews, and introduce access governance to enterprise resources. Veza particularly focuses on identity governance and administration (IGA) solutions, which are solutions that establish and manage the rules and processes governing access to systems.

Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Parties both supply IT security software products:

• ServiceNow supplies IT security software products known as security operations (SecOps) software and integrated risk management (IRM) software. SecOps and IRM products function as workflow management solutions and risk trackers for customers.

• Veza supplies IAM software security products which govern and monitor access to a customer’s IT systems.

While the Parties’ products can coexist within a customer’s technology environment, they operate separately and perform different functions for different purposes. As such, there is no horizontal overlap between the Parties in Australia.

There are no vertical relationships between the Parties in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form as well as further information provided by ServiceNow following pre-notification engagement with the ACCC.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market in Australia. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings:

• Veza has a very limited presence in Australia.

• There are no horizontal overlaps or vertical relationships between ServiceNow and Veza in Australia.

• The merged entity is unlikely to have the ability or incentive to foreclose Veza’s competitors in IAM (including IGA) by preventing their integration with ServiceNow, or to take other actions that would lead to conglomerate competition concerns in Australia. Also, ServiceNow’s offering relies on permitting integrations with a wide range of different platforms, tools and software in order to perform its functions and remain attractive to customers.

• ServiceNow’s primary focus is the supply of integrated IT workflow management software to enterprise customers. It faces competition from alternative providers of such software, including Microsoft, Broadcom, Oracle and Atlassian.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

NACG is a Canadian supplier of mining and heavy civil construction services to customers in the resource development, industrial construction and infrastructure sectors in Canada, the US, and Australia. Through its Australian subsidiaries, NACG supplies:

mergers.merger_description:
The proposed acquisition involves a consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (**Tega**) and the Apollo funds indirectly and collectively acquiring all outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that collectively form Molycop.

The Apollo Funds are managed by Apollo Global Management, Inc and indirect subsidiaries of it (**Apollo**).

The proposed acquisition will occur through a special purpose vehicle, Tega MC JV Holdings Pte. Ltd (**JV Co.**), which is indirectly and jointly controlled by Tega and the Apollo funds.  Tega indirectly holds 76.7% of JV Co., while Apollo indirectly hold the remaining 23.3%.

Tega is a global supplier of mining products such as mill and scrubber liners, trommels, screen media, hydrocyclones, slurry pumps and parts, conveyor components, chutes, and other rubber and composite wear parts. Tega also provides installation, condition monitoring, and maintenance services.

Apollo is an alternative asset management firm headquartered in New York. Examples of current investments include companies in natural resources, manufacturing and industrial, education, insurance, financial services, and leisure businesses.

Molycop is a global supplier of mining consumables, with a focus on the manufacture and sale of grinding media and flotation chemicals for use in both semi-autogenous grinding mills and ball mills. These products are used for mineral extraction and processing for copper, gold and iron ore. In Australia, Molycop also supplies engineered products for the Australian rail and manufacturing industries

events.title: Questionnaire - Tega/Apollo - Molycop
events.url_gh: /mergers/MN-85006/Apollo - Molycop_0_0.pdf

events.title: Tega and Apollo Funds consortium - Molycop - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: A consortium comprised of Tega Industries Limited (Tega) and funds advised and/or managed by affiliates of certain subsidiaries of Apollo Global Management, Inc (Apollo Funds) proposed acquisition of all outstanding shares in the subsidiaries of AIP MC Holdings LLC that collectively form Molycop (together, Molycop) (the Acquisition)]
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer is a consortium comprising Tega and the Apollo Funds.

Tega is a supplier of mining products such as mill and scrubber liners, trommels, screen media, hydrocyclones, slurry pumps and parts, conveyor components, chutes, and other rubber and composite wear parts. Tega also provides installation, condition monitoring, and maintenance services.

Apollo is an alternative asset management firm headquartered in New York. Examples of current investments include companies in natural resources, manufacturing and industrial, education, insurance, financial services, and leisure businesses.

The target, Molycop, is a global supplier of mining consumables, with a focus on the manufacture and sale of grinding media and flotation chemicals for use in both semi-autogenous grinding mills and ball mills.

These products are used for mineral extraction and processing of copper, gold, and iron ore. In Australia, Molycop also supplies engineered products for the Australian rail and manufacturing industries.

Molycop and Tega supply complementary products. Molycop’s grinding media and Tega’s mill liners are both inputs used by mining customers in grinding mills to process ore. Grinding media are fed into grinding mills together with ore. The rotation of the grinding mill creates impact between the grinding media and ore, reducing the ore material down to a finer size and liberating the minerals of value.

Key types of grinding media supplied by Molycop include:

• SAG balls: large, forged steel balls that operate with a semi-autogenous (SAG) mill – the first stage of grinding ore after crushing.

• Small / medium balls: smaller forged steel balls that operate a ball mill – the second phase of grinding ore (after the SAG mill).

• High-chrome balls: typically smaller balls (also used in a ball mill) with a higher chromium content, that have a lower wear rate than forged steel but are more susceptible to breakage.

Mill liners, such as those supplied by Tega, are customised wear parts that fit the interior grinding mills and ball mills to protect internal surfaces from wear and impact during grinding to extend a mill’s life and improve operational efficiency.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and information from third parties, and publicly available information.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings:

• There are no horizontal overlaps or material vertical relationships between the parties.

• The ACCC has considered the potential for conglomerate effects, particularly whether Tega would have the ability and incentive to leverage Molycop’s strong market position in grinding media to foreclose rival suppliers of mill liners by anti-competitively bundling or tying the two products. However, the ACCC found: − Many mining customers would be able to switch to a different supplier of grinding media in response to any tying or bundling strategy. While Molycop is the only supplier of Australian-made grinding media and has a very high market share, they are relatively commoditised products and customers are able to switch to imported products. − There would be practical barriers to bundling or tying the products. For example, customers typically procure mill liners and grinding media separately through different methods (spot purchases for mill liners and competitive tenders for grinding media) and over different time periods (grinding media are typically supplied under longer term supply arrangements).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Salesforce, Inc (**Salesforce**) proposes to acquire all of the outstanding voting securities of Qualified.com, Inc (**Qualified**).

Salesforce is a global supplier of customer relationship management (**CRM**) software and other software-as-a-service solutions that enable companies to manage and improve their relationships with customers. Salesforce’s core offerings are CRM solutions designed for the sales, service, marketing and commerce functions within a company. Salesforce, through Agentforce, also offers autonomous artificial intelligence (**AI**) agents that integrate across Salesforce’s services. Salesforce offers CRM and AI services to customers in Australia and abroad.

Qualified is a provider of the agentic marketing product “Piper”, which enables business-to-business (**B2B**) sales and marketing teams to improve customer prospecting and build a sales pipeline through AI-driven functionality. Piper is an inbound AI-based sales development representative (**SDR**) agent that was purpose-built to integrate with Salesforce’s CRM and uses Salesforce CRM data to deliver personalised experiences like custom greetings and targeted offers, while reporting customer engagement back into the Salesforce CRM system. Piper is Qualified’s only actively marketed product and is offered worldwide, including in Australia.

Both Salesforce and Qualified supply conversation automation solutions for B2B uses, which integrate with Salesforce’s CRM software. Conversation automation solutions for B2B uses includes tools, such as virtual assistants, AI agents, and chatbots, that support functions such as outbound and inbound sales prospecting for lead generation, customer qualification, customer research, meeting scheduling, and personalised product recommendations.

events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details: Salesforce, Inc’s (Salesforce)’s proposed acquisition of all of the outstanding voting securities of Qualified.com, Inc (Qualified) (the Acquisition).
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Salesforce, is a global supplier of customer relationship management (CRM) software and other software-as-a-service solutions that enable companies to manage and improve their relationships with customers.

Salesforce, through Agentforce, offers a suite of artificial intelligence (AI) agents, which are integrated with its core CRM offerings and automate tasks related to sales, service, marketing and commerce functions within a company. Offerings include personalised engagement with potential and existing customers and scheduling meetings. Salesforce offers CRM and AI services to customers in Australia and abroad.

The target, Qualified, is a provider of an AI-based sales development representative (SDR) agent, known as “Piper”, which enables business-to-business (B2B) sales and marketing teams to improve customer prospecting and build a sales pipeline through AI-driven functionality. Piper was purpose-built to integrate with Salesforce’s CRM software and uses Salesforce CRM data to deliver personalised experiences like custom greetings and targeted offers, while reporting customer engagement back into the Salesforce CRM system. Piper is Qualified’s only actively marketed product and is offered worldwide, including in Australia.

determination_table_content.item: Overlap and relationship between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Salesforce and Qualified (together, the Parties) overlap in Australia in the supply of conversation automation solutions for B2B uses, such as virtual assistants, AI agents and chatbots, to support sales and marketing teams to engage with customers. Salesforce’s Agentforce offering includes an outbound business development representative, which is focused on generating new leads from those that have not yet expressed interest in a company. Qualified’s Piper is an inbound SDR focused on engaging prospective customers that have already expressed interest in a company.

In addition to the horizontal overlap, there is a vertical relationship between Qualified’s product Piper and Salesforce’s CRM platform.

Piper uses Salesforce’s CRM data to inform its agent’s interactions and records information about customer engagement in Salesforce’s CRM platform.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form and information from third partes.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.

• there is limited horizontal overlap between Salesforce and Qualified in the supply of conversation automation services in Australia, as the products the Parties supply target customers at different points in the sales and marketing process. In addition, Qualified has a small presence in Australia.

• the merged entity would continue to face competition from alternative suppliers of conversation automation solutions, in Australia and internationally.

• while there is a vertical relationship between the products supplied by the Parties, the merged entity would be unlikely to have the ability and incentive to foreclose rivals’ access to CRM services given there are alternative suppliers of CRM services in Australia and internationally. Salesforce may be able to offer bundled or tied offerings to some customers, but we do not consider this potential gives rise to conglomerate concerns.

Salesforce submits that a foundational characteristic of its platform is it being open and interoperable, which limits incentive it may have to foreclose rivals’ access.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any application for review, please contact the Australian Competition Tribunal.

mergers.merger_description:
Banijay Group N.V., a listed company in Euronext Amsterdam, (together with the rest of the Banijay group, **Banijay Group**) and RedBird IMI (a joint venture between RedBird Capital Partners and IMI Media Group) propose to combine Banijay Group's content production and distribution business and part of its live event business and RedBird's All3Media into a newly incorporated company, Banijay Holding UK Limited (**CombinedCo**) (the **Acquisition**).

Post-Acquisition, Banijay Group will hold 50% of CombinedCo’s share capital and voting rights, and will have sole control of CombinedCo. Gold Rush TopCo Limited and an affiliate of funds advised by affiliates of RedBird together will hold the other 50% of CombinedCo’s share capital and voting rights.

Post-Acquisition, Banijay Group’s Banijay Entertainment business, and part of Banijay Live's business would form part of CombinedCo, along with All3Media.

Banijay Entertainment focuses on the production and distribution of audiovisual content with over 130 production companies.

In Australia, Banijay Entertainment is active through its subsidiary, Endemol Shine Australia. Endemol Shine Australia’s productions are broadcast across Australian free-to-air networks and their broadcast video on demand offerings, as well as subscription TV and subscription video on demand services. They include shows such as MasterChef, Australian Survivor, Big Brother, LEGO Masters, Married at First Sight, as well as dramas including NCIS: Sydney and Offspring. Banijay Live's business does not operate in Australia.

All3Media is a UK-based company active in the production and distribution of audiovisual content. All3Media’s current activities in Australia are:

* the distribution of content to broadcasters and streamers in Australia through All3Media International
* the production of one television program for Australian broadcaster Ten via its New Zealand subsidiary, South Pacific Pictures; and
* Little Dot Studios, a social media agency.

CARe Autobody Pty Ltd (**CARe**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital in MotorOne Autobody Pty Ltd (**MotorOne Autobody**) (the **Acquisition**).

CARe is an Australian supplier of motor vehicle collision repair services. CARe operates a total of 18 repair sites across Australia with 5 sites in Queensland, 7 sites in Victoria, 3 sites in New South Wales, 2 sites in Tasmania and 1 site in South Australia.

MotorOne Autobody is an Australian supplier of motor vehicle collision repair services and a business division of the MotorOne Group which is an Australian supplier of motor vehicle aftercare products and services. MotorOne Autobody operates a total of 20 repair sites in Australia with 8 sites in Queensland, 11 sites in Victoria and 1 site in New South Wales.

Both CARe and MotorOne Autobody supply motor vehicle collision repair services to insurers and direct to vehicle owners without an insurance claim.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the interests of consumers.

For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines and interim merger process guidelines.

In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered the information and documents that were submitted with the notification form, publicly available information, and feedback from market participants.

The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC makes the following findings.
